title: UK's Healthy Life Expectancy Drops by Two Years
summary: A report from the Health Foundation reveals that healthy life expectancy (HLE) in the UK has fallen by about two years over the past decade, now just under 61 years for both men and women. This decline places the UK among only five of the 21 richest countries where HLE has decreased, and it has the second steepest fall. The analysis, based on Office for National Statistics data from 2012-2014 and 2022-2024, highlights stark inequalities: those in the wealthiest 10% of areas enjoy about 20 more years of good health than those in the poorest 10%. For instance, Richmond, London, has an HLE of 69 for men and 70 for women, while Blackpool's men have only 51 years and Hartlepool's women 51 years. London was the only region to see improvement. The report attributes the decline to poverty, poor housing, obesity, mental ill health, and the COVID-19 pandemic. It warns that HLE is now below the state pension age (66 or 67) in over 90% of areas, affecting people's ability to work. The UK ranks 20th out of 21 rich countries, ahead of only the US. The Health Foundation calls this a watershed moment for policymakers.

keywords.explanation: An estimate of how many years a person lives in good health, based on surveys and death data.
Unfair differences between groups of people, such as in health or wealth.
The age at which people can start receiving a pension from the government, currently 66 or 67 in the UK.

questions.question: How much has healthy life expectancy in the UK dropped over the past decade?
What is the current healthy life expectancy for both men and women in the UK?
Among the 21 richest countries, how many have seen a decline in HLE?
How many more years of good health do people in the wealthiest areas have compared to the poorest?
Which region in the UK saw an improvement in HLE?
What is the HLE for men in Blackpool?
What percentage of areas have HLE below the state pension age?
Which country has a worse HLE than the UK among the 21 richest?

background_read: Healthy life expectancy (HLE) is a key indicator of a population's health. It combines mortality data with self-reported health status. The UK has seen a worrying decline, especially compared to other wealthy nations. Factors like poverty, housing, and lifestyle are major contributors. The report emphasizes the economic impact, as poor health reduces workforce participation. Policymakers are urged to address these issues urgently.
Article_Structure: The article reports a decline in UK healthy life expectancy (HLE) by two years to under 61. Main points: HLE drop, regional inequalities (e.g., Richmond vs. Blackpool), and global ranking (20th out of 21). Purpose: To highlight a public health crisis and urge policy action. Evidence: ONS data comparing 2012-14 and 2022-24, WHO global comparison. Author credibility: Health Foundation is a reputable think tank. Methodology: Analysis of ONS and WHO data, with comparisons across regions and countries.

perspectives.description: The think tank argues that the decline is a 'watershed moment' requiring government action.
They might say that austerity and underfunding of public services have contributed to the decline.
